title: Grid System
question: How does Bootstrap's grid system work?
answer: 12-column responsive grid using .container, .row, and .col-* classes with breakpoints (sm, md, lg, xl, xxl).
explanation: It's built on Flexbox under the hood.
codeExample:
A
B
bestPractices: Use gutters (.g-*) instead of manual margins.
commonMistakes: Nesting containers redundantly.
interviewTips: Explain container-fluid vs container.

title: Utility Classes
question: What are Bootstrap utility classes?
answer: Single-purpose classes like m-3, p-2, text-center, d-flex that apply one CSS property.
explanation: They speed up prototyping and reduce custom CSS.
codeExample: ...
bestPractices: Combine utilities with a few semantic classes.
commonMistakes: Utility soup with no semantic structure.
interviewTips: Compare to Tailwind's philosophy.

title: Customization with SCSS
question: How do you customize Bootstrap 5?
answer: Override SCSS variables (e.g. $primary, $body-bg) before importing bootstrap's source, then compile.
explanation: The Bootstrap docs list every variable you can override.
codeExample:
$primary: #4f46e5;
@import "bootstrap/scss/bootstrap";
bestPractices: Only import the modules you use.
commonMistakes: Overriding after import (won't work).
interviewTips: Show modular imports.

title: Components
question: Name a few Bootstrap JS components.
answer: Modals, Dropdowns, Toasts, Offcanvas, Carousel, Collapse, Tooltip, Popover.
explanation: Many require initialising via data attributes or JS constructors.
codeExample: <button data-bs-toggle="modal" data-bs-target="#m">Open</button>
bestPractices: Prefer data attributes for simple cases.
commonMistakes: Missing the JS bundle for interactive components.
interviewTips: Mention Popper.js dependency for tooltips.

title: Breakpoints & Responsive Utilities
question: What are Bootstrap 5 breakpoints and how do responsive utilities use them?
answer: Bootstrap ships six breakpoints (xs <576px, sm ≥576, md ≥768, lg ≥992, xl ≥1200, xxl ≥1400). Responsive utilities embed the breakpoint infix (e.g. d-none d-md-block) to apply styles from that width up.
explanation: Breakpoints are min-width and mobile-first: a class with no infix applies to all sizes, and an infix overrides it from that breakpoint upward. xs has no infix because it's the default. This lets you show, hide, or restyle elements per viewport without media queries.
codeExample:
Visible from md up
Hidden on lg and larger

Centered, left-aligned on lg+
bestPractices: Design mobile-first: set the base then override upward.
Use only the breakpoints you need to keep markup lean.
commonMistakes: Assuming breakpoints are max-width (they are min-width).
Adding an xs infix like d-xs-block (xs has no infix).
interviewTips: List the six breakpoints and their pixel thresholds.
Explain why the framework is mobile-first.

title: Flexbox Utilities
question: How do Bootstrap flexbox utilities help you build layouts?
answer: Classes like d-flex, flex-row/column, justify-content-*, align-items-*, flex-wrap, and flex-grow-* expose CSS flexbox properties directly in markup, and most accept breakpoint infixes.
explanation: Instead of writing custom flex CSS you compose behaviour from utilities: d-flex enables the flex container, justify-content-* controls the main axis, align-items-* the cross axis, and flex-fill/flex-grow-* distribute space. They compose with the grid for fine-grained control.
codeExample:
Logo
Home About
bestPractices: Use gap-* utilities instead of margins between flex children.
Combine responsive infixes to switch axis per breakpoint.
commonMistakes: Forgetting d-flex, so justify-content-* has no effect.
Confusing justify-content (main axis) with align-items (cross axis).
interviewTips: Explain main vs cross axis in flexbox terms.
Mention gap-* as a modern spacing approach.

title: Spacing Utilities
question: How does Bootstrap's spacing utility notation work?
answer: The format is {property}{sides}-{breakpoint}-{size}: property m (margin) or p (padding); sides t/b/s/e/x/y or blank; size 0–5 or auto based on the $spacer scale.
explanation: Spacing utilities map to a spacer of 1rem by default; size 3 = 1rem, 1 = 0.25rem, 5 = 3rem. Sides use logical properties: s = start (left in LTR), e = end. Negative margins (mt-n1) and auto (ms-auto) are also supported.
codeExample: Spaced box
bestPractices: Use logical s/e sides so spacing flips correctly in RTL.
Lean on the spacer scale instead of arbitrary custom margins.
commonMistakes: Using l/r (Bootstrap 4) instead of s/e (Bootstrap 5).
Forgetting ms-auto/me-auto to push flex items.
interviewTips: Decode a class like px-md-4 on the spot.
Explain the $spacer variable and how sizes scale.

title: Container Types
question: What container types does Bootstrap 5 provide?
answer: .container is fixed-width per breakpoint, .container-fluid is 100% wide always, and .container-{breakpoint} is fluid until the named breakpoint then fixed.
explanation: Responsive containers (container-sm, container-md, etc.) stay 100% wide until the viewport reaches that breakpoint, after which they adopt the fixed max-width. This gives edge-to-edge layouts on small screens with a constrained reading width on large ones.
codeExample:
Fixed width per breakpoint
Always full width
Fluid until lg, then fixed
bestPractices: Wrap grid rows in a container to apply gutter padding correctly.
Pick container-{bp} for hero sections that go edge-to-edge on mobile.
commonMistakes: Nesting a container inside another container.
Using container-fluid then re-adding max-width in custom CSS.
interviewTips: Contrast fixed, fluid, and responsive containers.
Explain how containers interact with the grid gutters.

title: Cards
question: How do you build a Bootstrap card component?
answer: Use .card as the wrapper with .card-header, .card-img-top, .card-body (containing .card-title, .card-text), and .card-footer to compose a flexible content container.
explanation: Cards are content containers built on flexbox, so they align nicely in grids and card groups. Sub-elements are optional and can be mixed freely, and utility classes control spacing, borders, and shadows.
codeExample:
Card title

Some quick example text.

Go
bestPractices: Use .h-100 on cards inside a row for equal heights.
Add alt text on card-img-top for accessibility.
commonMistakes: Omitting .card-body so padding collapses.
Setting fixed pixel widths instead of using the grid.
interviewTips: List the common card sub-components.
Explain how cards align in a responsive grid.

title: Navbar
question: How do you create a responsive Bootstrap navbar?
answer: Use .navbar with a responsive .navbar-expand-{breakpoint}, a .navbar-brand, a .navbar-toggler for the collapsed menu, and a collapsible .collapse.navbar-collapse containing .navbar-nav.
explanation: The navbar collapses into a hamburger toggler below the chosen expand breakpoint and shows a horizontal menu above it. The toggler uses collapse via data-bs-toggle/data-bs-target, so the Bootstrap JS bundle is required for the toggle to work.
codeExample:
Brand
  • Home
bestPractices: Match the navbar-expand breakpoint to your menu length.
Use aria-controls and aria-expanded on the toggler for accessibility.
commonMistakes: Mismatched toggler target id and collapse id.
Forgetting navbar-expand-* so the menu never expands.
interviewTips: Explain how the toggler ties into the collapse component.
Mention the JS bundle dependency.

title: Modals
question: How do Bootstrap modals work and how are they triggered?
answer: A .modal container holds .modal-dialog > .modal-content with header/body/footer. Trigger it declaratively with data-bs-toggle="modal" data-bs-target="#id" or programmatically via new bootstrap.Modal(el).show().
explanation: Modals are positioned over everything with a backdrop, and only one can be open at a time. Focus is trapped inside for accessibility. The Bootstrap JS bundle handles show/hide, backdrop clicks, and the Escape key.
codeExample:
Title
Body content
bestPractices: Add tabindex="-1" and aria-labelledby for accessibility.
Use data-bs-backdrop="static" to prevent accidental dismissal of important dialogs.
commonMistakes: Placing the modal markup inside overflow-hidden containers.
Forgetting the JS bundle so the modal never opens.
interviewTips: Explain focus trapping and the backdrop.
Show both data-attribute and JS triggering.

title: Forms & Validation Styles
question: How does Bootstrap style forms and provide validation feedback?
answer: Form controls use .form-control, .form-select, .form-check, and .form-label. Validation uses .was-validated on the form (with :valid/:invalid) or explicit .is-valid/.is-invalid classes plus .valid-feedback/.invalid-feedback.
explanation: Bootstrap ships both browser-native constraint validation (add .needs-validation + novalidate and toggle .was-validated with JS) and server-side style hooks via .is-invalid. Feedback elements only display when the matching state class is present.
codeExample: Please enter a valid email.
bestPractices: Always pair inputs with a .form-label for accessibility.
Provide both valid and invalid feedback messages.
commonMistakes: Expecting feedback to show without .was-validated or .is-invalid.
Omitting novalidate, so the browser's default UI overrides Bootstrap's.
interviewTips: Contrast client-side .was-validated with server-side .is-invalid.
Mention accessibility of labels and feedback.

title: Buttons & Button Groups
question: What button styles and grouping options does Bootstrap offer?
answer: .btn with a variant (.btn-primary, .btn-outline-secondary, etc.), sizing (.btn-lg/.btn-sm), states (disabled, active), and .btn-group / .btn-toolbar for grouping related buttons.
explanation: Button variants map to theme colors and their outline versions. Button groups visually merge adjacent buttons and can act as radio/checkbox toggles when combined with .btn-check inputs, useful for segmented controls.
bestPractices: Add role="group" and aria-label to button groups.
Use .btn-check with labels for accessible toggle groups.
commonMistakes: Using <a> without role="button" for actions.
Only styling with .btn-primary and omitting the base .btn class.
interviewTips: Explain solid vs outline variants.
Describe how .btn-check builds toggle buttons.

title: Badges & Alerts
question: How do you use Bootstrap badges and alerts?
answer: Badges (.badge with a bg-* color) are small count/label indicators, while alerts (.alert.alert-*) are contextual feedback banners that can be dismissible with .alert-dismissible and a .btn-close.
explanation: Badges scale with their parent's font size and pair well with buttons or headings. Alerts convey success/warning/danger messages; the dismissible variant needs the JS bundle to fade out when the close button is clicked.
codeExample:
Inbox 9

Session expiring soon.
bestPractices: Add role="alert" so screen readers announce messages.
Use .visually-hidden text for badge context (e.g. 'unread').
commonMistakes: Forgetting data-bs-dismiss="alert" on the close button.
Relying on color alone to convey alert meaning.
interviewTips: Explain contextual color variants.
Mention accessibility roles for alerts.

title: Dropdowns
question: How do Bootstrap dropdowns work?
answer: A .dropdown wrapper contains a toggle button with data-bs-toggle="dropdown" and a .dropdown-menu of .dropdown-item links. Positioning is handled by Popper.js.
explanation: Dropdowns rely on Popper to place the menu relative to the toggle and flip it when there isn't room. They work on buttons or links, support split buttons, dividers (.dropdown-divider), and headers, and require the Bootstrap JS bundle (which includes Popper).
codeExample:
• Action
  • Separated
bestPractices: Use the bundle build so Popper is included.
Add aria attributes for keyboard and screen-reader support.
commonMistakes: Loading bootstrap.js without Popper for the standalone build.
Missing data-bs-toggle on the toggle element.
interviewTips: Explain Popper's role in positioning.
Mention split-button dropdown markup.

title: Offcanvas
question: What is the Bootstrap offcanvas component used for?
answer: .offcanvas is a hidden sidebar panel that slides in from an edge (start, end, top, bottom), toggled with data-bs-toggle="offcanvas" and containing .offcanvas-header and .offcanvas-body.
explanation: Offcanvas is ideal for mobile menus, filter panels, and carts. It behaves like a modal with a backdrop but slides from a screen edge. Placement classes (offcanvas-start/end/top/bottom) control the direction, and it needs the JS bundle.
codeExample:
Filters
Content
bestPractices: Use offcanvas as a responsive navbar alternative on mobile.
Provide a labelled close button for accessibility.
commonMistakes: Mismatched data-bs-target and the offcanvas id.
Expecting it to work without the JS bundle.
interviewTips: Compare offcanvas to a modal.
Explain the four placement options.

title: Accordion & Collapse
question: How do the collapse and accordion components relate in Bootstrap?
answer: Collapse toggles visibility of a target via data-bs-toggle="collapse". An accordion (.accordion) is a group of collapsible items sharing a data-bs-parent so opening one closes the others.
explanation: The accordion is built on top of the collapse plugin. Each .accordion-item has a header button targeting an .accordion-collapse; the data-bs-parent attribute enforces the exclusive open behaviour. Remove data-bs-parent to allow multiple panels open at once.
codeExample: Answer
bestPractices: Use data-bs-parent to keep only one panel open when desired.
Keep header text inside a heading element for structure.
commonMistakes: Forgetting data-bs-parent, so accordion items don't auto-close.
Duplicate target ids across items.
interviewTips: Explain that accordion is collapse plus a parent.
Show how to allow multiple open panels.

title: Tooltips & Popovers (Popper)
question: How do you enable Bootstrap tooltips and popovers?
answer: Both are opt-in for performance: add data-bs-toggle="tooltip"/"popover" with a title, then initialise them in JS (e.g. new bootstrap.Tooltip(el)). They use Popper for positioning.
explanation: Unlike most components, tooltips and popovers are not auto-initialised because attaching listeners to every element would be costly. You must select the elements and instantiate them. Popover adds a title and body; tooltip shows only a title on hover/focus.
codeExample:
<button class="btn btn-secondary" data-bs-toggle="tooltip" title="Hi there">Hover</button>
<script>
  const list = document.querySelectorAll('[data-bs-toggle="tooltip"]');
  [...list].forEach(el => new bootstrap.Tooltip(el));
</script>
bestPractices: Initialise only the elements that need tooltips.
Use data-bs-container="body" to avoid clipping inside overflow containers.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to initialise them in JS.
Relying on tooltips alone to convey essential information.
interviewTips: Explain why they're opt-in for performance.
Mention Popper positioning and the sanitize option for HTML content.

title: Carousel
question: How do you build a Bootstrap carousel?
answer: Use .carousel with .carousel-inner holding .carousel-item slides (one .active), plus optional controls (.carousel-control-prev/next) and indicators. Configure via data-bs-ride="carousel" or the JS Carousel API.
explanation: The carousel cycles through slides automatically when data-bs-ride is set. Controls provide manual navigation and indicators show position. Options like interval, pause, and wrap can be set through data attributes or the constructor, and it needs the JS bundle.
bestPractices: Provide alt text on slide images for accessibility.
Consider pausing autoplay on hover/focus for usability.
commonMistakes: Forgetting exactly one .active slide.
Omitting w-100 so images don't fill the slide.
interviewTips: Explain data-bs-ride vs manual control.
Mention accessibility concerns with auto-rotation.

title: Dark Mode, CSS Variables, RTL & v4→v5 Migration
question: How does Bootstrap 5.3 support dark mode, CSS variables, RTL, and what changed from v4 to v5?
answer: Bootstrap 5.3 adds color modes via data-bs-theme="dark" on any element, exposes CSS custom properties (--bs-*) for runtime theming, ships an RTL build using logical properties, and v5 dropped jQuery, renamed left/right utilities to start/end, and switched data-* to data-bs-*.
explanation: Setting data-bs-theme="dark" swaps the CSS variables that drive colors, so you can theme the whole page or a single component. The --bs-* variables let you override styles at runtime without recompiling SCSS. RTL is enabled with dir="rtl" plus the bootstrap.rtl.css build. Migrating from v4 mainly means updating utility names (ml→ms, mr→me), data attribute prefixes, and removing the jQuery dependency.
codeExample: Dark, RTL, variable-themed
bestPractices: Scope data-bs-theme to sections for mixed light/dark UIs.
Prefer overriding --bs-* variables for runtime theming over recompiling.
commonMistakes: Leaving v4 ml-*/mr-* classes after upgrading to v5.
Forgetting to load the .rtl build when using dir="rtl".
interviewTips: Summarise the top v4→v5 breaking changes.
Explain how color modes leverage CSS variables.
